pattern_recognition: Tree Traversal, because the problem involves navigating through a binary tree structure and modifying it. The signals are the need to swap child nodes at each level of the tree.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Recursively traverse the tree and swap the left and right children at each node.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Start from the root node. 2. Swap the left and right children. 3. Recursively apply the same process to the left and right children.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a level-order traversal (BFS) to swap the children of each node iteratively.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Use a queue to perform level-order traversal. 2. For each node, swap its left and right children. 3. Enqueue the children for further processing.

dry_run: Given the input level-order: [4, 2, 7, 1, 3, 6, 9]. Start with the root (4), swap its children (2 and 7). Then move to 2, swap its children (1 and 3), and move to 7, swap its children (6 and 9). The inverted level-order will be [4, 7, 2, 9, 6, 3, 1].
edge_cases: An empty input should return an empty tree. A single element input should return the same single element. Duplicates should be handled as normal nodes. Large inputs may require consideration of memory limits. Overflow is not a concern for tree structures but should be noted for node values.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to check for null nodes before swapping can lead to null pointer exceptions. 2. Not handling the base case in recursion can cause infinite recursion. 3. Confusing the order of left and right children during the swap can lead to incorrect tree structure.
